attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old woman is undergoing an urgent laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for colorectal cancer. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

During preoperative assessment, she appears well with normal vital signs - pulse rate 70/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 12/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C.

Due to the prolonged and invasive nature of the procedure, the anaesthetist inserts an arterial line prior to the procedure for invasive blood pressure monitoring. Other monitoring is used as standard, with continuous 3 lead ECG, pulse oximetry, end-tidal capnography post intubation, and monitoring of airway pressures and volumes via the ventilator.

The patient is intubated without issue, and the surgery begins with the creation of port sites and peritoneal insufflation with carbon dioxide to a pressure of 16 mmHg.

The anaesthetist is watching their monitor and notices several immediate physiological changes during establishment of surgical pneumoperitoneum. They have not yet altered any settings on the ventilator nor given any further medications.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely physiological change expected during this stage of the anaesthetic and surgery?
attributes.explanation:
Peritoneal insufflation with carbon dioxide during laparoscopic surgery causes significant physiological changes. The increased intra-abdominal pressure affects multiple organ systems, particularly cardiovascular and respiratory function.

Decreased venous return is the most likely immediate physiological change during insufflation. The increased intra-abdominal pressure compresses the inferior vena cava, reducing venous return to the heart. This leads to decreased cardiac output and may require fluid loading or positioning changes to maintain haemodynamic stability.

Increased cardiac output would not occur as the increased intra-abdominal pressure impedes venous return. While there may be an initial increase in cardiac output due to compression of splanchnic vessels, this is typically followed by a decrease due to reduced preload.

Decreased systemic vascular resistance is incorrect as pneumoperitoneum typically causes an increase in systemic vascular resistance due to mechanical compression of vessels and neurohumoral responses.

Decreased peak airway pressure would not occur as the elevated diaphragm and increased intra-abdominal pressure actually lead to increased airway pressures. This often requires adjustment of ventilator settings.

Increased functional residual capacity is incorrect as pneumoperitoneum causes cephalad displacement of the diaphragm, reducing lung volumes including functional residual capacity. This can lead to atelectasis and impaired gas exchange.
learningPoints: Peritoneal insufflation with carbon dioxide causes increased intra-abdominal pressure leading to decreased venous return and cardiac output.
The increased intra-abdominal pressure from insufflation causes elevation of the diaphragm, reducing functional residual capacity.
Absorption of carbon dioxide across the peritoneum can lead to hypercarbia requiring increased minute ventilation.
references: C.N. Gutt, T. Oniu, A. Mehrabi, P. Schemmer, A. Kashfi, T. Kraus, M.W. Büchler; Circulatory and Respiratory Complications of Carbon Dioxide Insufflation. Dig Surg 1 March 2004; 21 (2): 95–105. https://doi.org/10.1159/000077038

Safran, David B. et al. Physiologic effects of pneumoperitoneum. The American Journal of Surgery, Volume 167, Issue 2, 281 - 286. Available at: https://www.americanjournalofsurgery.com/article/0002-9610(94)90094-9/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
